FCX operates at 35–45% EBITDA margins across copper cycles. For Brimstone Plant 1 at startup: 20–25% is realistic in year 1–2; 30–35% by year 3–5; 38–42% at steady state.

## C. Unit Economics — The Alumina Co-Production Cost Advantage

If Plant 1 total operating cost is estimated at ~$13–14M/year at full ramp (rough estimate: $160/t cement-equivalent across 100,000t combined output including alumina), and cost is allocated on a mass basis:

| Product | Volume | Mass % | Allocated OpEx | Unit Cost | Market Price | Unit Margin |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cement | 80,000 t | 80% | ~$10.4–11.2M | **$130–140/t** | $140–155/t | $0–25/t |
| Alumina | 20,000 t | 20% | ~$2.6–2.8M | **$130–140/t** | $460–520/t | **$320–390/t** |
| SCM | 15,000 t | — | Marginal | ~$20–30/t | $65/t | $35–45/t |

The alumina unit margin of $320–390/t at base pricing, at 20,000t/year production, generates approximately **$6.4–7.8M per year in margin on the alumina line alone** — on a total-plant EBITDA of $6.5–8.7M. The cement line operates near breakeven by itself; the alumina line generates essentially all the platform's economic surplus. This is not a cement company with an alumina side business — it is an alumina business that uses cement production to defray the fixed cost of mining and processing.

Compare to the FCX gold byproduct mechanic: FCX's copper mines generate their primary economic return from copper; the gold and molybdenum byproducts, extracted at near-marginal cost, account for the majority of free cash flow generation in high-price environments. The structural parallel is exact.

# PART VIII: THE ASTM C150 MOAT — CONFIRMED FINAL ANALYSIS

## What C150 Requires


ASTM C150/C150M covers eight types of portland cement. The cement covered by this specification shall only contain the following ingredients: portland cement clinker; water or calcium sulfate, or both; limestone; processing additions; and air-entraining addition for air-entraining portland cement. Portland cement of each of the eight types must have specific chemical compositions including: aluminum oxide, ferric oxide, magnesium oxide, sulfur trioxide, tricalcium silicate, dicalcium silicate, tricalcium aluminate, and tetracalcium aluminoferrite.


## Amazon Validation — The Key Commercial Stress Test


In August 2025, Brimstone announced that Amazon had signed a commercial agreement to reserve annual volumes of Brimstone's portland cement and SCM from its forthcoming plant. This agreement builds on successful initial testing showing that Brimstone cement performs comparably well with the conventionally produced materials used in Amazon's buildings.


Amazon's construction procurement team validates materials against real structural performance requirements across billions of dollars of construction annually. "Performs comparably well" is engineering procurement language for specification compliance — not a marketing claim.

## Moat Duration Estimate

A competitor seeking to replicate ASTM C150 from a non-limestone feedstock would need: (1) a novel feedstock with correct Ca:Al:Si:Fe stoichiometry, (2) multi-year pilot testing, (3) independent third-party certification across multiple test cycles including three-day heat of hydration testing conducted at least once every six months, plus long-term strength testing at 1, 3, 7, and 28 days, and (4) customer qualification and offtake development. Minimum competitive replication timeline: **5–8 years from feedstock identification**.

## Strategic Value for Infrastructure Buildout


U.S. government agencies, including the U.S. Departments of Homeland Security, Treasury, Energy, and Commerce, have repeatedly recognized alumina as critical to U.S. strategic interests. Under the Trump Administration, the Department of Commerce issued a report highlighting the national security implications of foreign aluminum production.
 ASTM C150-certified cement from domestic sources is the cement specification required for all federally funded infrastructure — the same political tailwind that protects the alumina business also protects the cement business.

# PART IX: STOICHIOMETRIC ANALYSIS — FINAL POSITION

## The Rock Determines the Product Mix


Brimstone has signed a letter of intent with Oklahoma's Dolese Bros. Co. to supply raw material for the proposed Rock Refinery, which will co-produce smelter-grade alumina, Portland cement, and supplementary cementitious materials (SCM). Brimstone selected Dolese Bros. following a competitive review of over 23,000 quarries across the country.


The selection of one specific quarry from 23,000+ evaluated strongly implies that the Roosevelt quarry feedrock has a specific geochemical composition that makes Brimstone's process economically viable — and that not every rock body can serve as feedstock. The Ca:Al:Si:Mg:Fe ratios of the Roosevelt quarry anorthosite are geologically fixed, which means the 80,000t cement / 20,000t alumina / ~15,000t SCM output split is *stoichiometrically determined by the rock*, not by operator choice.

**The "downsizing" from 140,000t to 80,000t cement narrative is definitively resolved:** The original DOE March 2024 announcement described 140,000t/yr of "green cement." The January 2025 announcement explicitly broke out 80,000t cement + 20,000t alumina — with the same $378M plant cost. This is a reclassification of previously aggregated product tonnes into their separate high-value components, not a reduction in total mineral output. It is value-accretive: 20,000t previously counted as "cement" at $140/t is now correctly counted as alumina at $480/t — a $6.8M annual revenue reclassification upward, not a reduction.

Together, they bring more than 60 years of combined experience across the other three great refineries — Rens in scaling companies, including raising nearly $20B across fertilizer and oil, and Uhrie in deploying new billion-dollar plants for copper-containing complex metal sulfides.

A company hiring a $20B-raise CFO and a complex-metal-sulfide plant builder is not preparing a climate-tech venture round. It is preparing a large industrial capital raise — likely $200–300M+ — at industrial/infrastructure valuation multiples. The Brimstone board and investors understand that the valuation frame they are establishing matters enormously: a $200M infrastructure equity raise at 12x forward EBITDA requires a fundamentally different investor presentation than a $50M climate VC round at 5x revenue.

The addition of Kajima Ventures (not disclosed in the June 2025 investor list, appearing for the first time in the December 2025 list) confirms Japanese strategic capital is entering — the construction industry establishment beginning to invest in its own disruptor.

# PART XIV: THE MISVALUATION THESIS — FINAL POSITION

## The Three Types of Wrong Investors

**Type 1: Climate VC (3–5x revenue) — Undervalues by 3–5x**
Applies a climate-tech startup multiple. Gets to $65–110M on Plant 1 revenue. Misses alumina economics entirely. Misses EGA/Century demand validation. Misses regulatory moat value. Misses platform operating leverage. This is the dominant frame in Brimstone's current investor base — and it is why the company is hiring a CFO who has raised $20B in oil and fertilizer, not another climate VC general partner.

**Type 2: Cement Industry Strategic (7–8x EBITDA) — Undervalues by 2–3x**
A CRH, Holcim, or Vulcan applying a pure cement multiple. Gets to $53M on base EBITDA. Radically undervalues the alumina business (which generates essentially all the economic surplus), the C150 regulatory moat (which any cement major would pay dearly to own), and the EGA/Century demand alignment (which is a once-in-50-year domestic alumina market opportunity). The strategic premium over financial value is enormous.

**Type 3: Infrastructure/Critical Minerals Investor (12–15x EBITDA) — Correctly Values at $175–285M**
Understands the multi-commodity refinery structure, applies FCX/Iluka/mineral sands comparable framework, recognizes the EGA/Century demand as the transformative commercial de-risking event, and arrives at $175–285M enterprise value range. This is the investor Brimstone is now explicitly targeting with the CFO hire and the "Fourth Great Refinery" branding.

| **5+ plants, full product roadmap** | MP Materials / Lynas (critical minerals platform, national security importance) | 18–22x | **$500M–$1B+** |

## THE ONE-PARAGRAPH INVESTMENT THESIS

Brimstone Energy is building the only industrial process in the world that: (1) holds an ASTM C150 certification for cement produced from non-limestone feedstock — the mandatory specification for every federally funded road, bridge, and building in the United States, achieved after years of independent testing that no competitor can replicate for 5–8 years; (2) produces smelter-grade alumina domestically for the first time in a generation, at an estimated cost of $130–140/t vs. $460–520/t market price in Oklahoma, creating a structural margin of $320–390/t on 20,000 tonnes per year at Plant 1; (3) is located in Oklahoma, the same state where EGA and Century Aluminum — backed by a $200B U.S.-UAE White House commercial agreement and Bechtel engineering — are building the first new U.S. aluminum smelter since 1980, creating a structural domestic alumina demand deficit of ~1.05 million tonnes per year against which Brimstone's 20,000 t/yr output is immediately and fully absorbed; and (4) has hired a CFO who raised $20B for oil and fertilizer platforms and a VP Engineering from billion-dollar complex metal sulfide plants — explicitly signaling a large industrial capital raise at refinery-platform multiples, not a climate VC round. An investor applying a cement company multiple (7x EBITDA, $53M) to this platform is pricing in only the primary product while ignoring the entire economic engine. The correct comparable framework — FCX/Iluka/Mineral sands co-production platform — implies an enterprise value of $175–285M today, with a milestone-based re-rating path to $500M–$1B+ at 5+ plant scale. The platform multiple premium over single-product cement valuation is **2.6–5.0x** — and the primary catalyst for realizing it is a signed alumina offtake agreement with EGA/Century Inola, which is now the highest-probability near-term value creation event in Brimstone's corporate development roadmap.

The ACPF has invested in companies including Rivian (IPO'd 2021, S-1 contains detailed ACPF investment terms), CarbonCure Technologies, Pachama, Turntide Technologies, Infinium, and Brimstone. CRH plc — the most natural cement-sector acquirer of Brimstone — has separately invested in Sublime Systems (Brimstone's direct competitor in green cement). Separately, EGA/Mubadala (potential aluminum-sector investor) would likely face fewer Amazon objections, as there is no competitive conflict. HYPOTHESIS TO INVESTIGATE:
Amazon's quad-role creates a structural veto that shapes the buyer universe in any M&A process. Standard ACPF investment terms likely include change-of-control consent rights (estimated 80%+ probability based on CVC norms), meaning Amazon could block or condition any acquisition. A cement major acquisition (CRH, Amrize) would likely trigger scrutiny due to competitive conflicts and supply chain concerns; an aluminum-sector investment (EGA) or infrastructure fund would likely receive consent more easily. This structural constraint is not priced into any buyer's approach analysis and could add 3–6 months to an M&A timeline.

SPECIFIC RESEARCH QUESTIONS:

3. What is the legal interaction between Amazon's equity position and its commercial reservation agreement? 5. Could Amazon's reservation agreement contain a change-of-control triggered conversion from non-binding reservation to binding take-or-pay — a "poison pill" that actually enhances bankability by creating contracted revenue? Is this a standard provision in CVC-linked commercial agreements?

RESEARCH GUIDANCE:

- SEC EDGAR: Any ACPF portfolio company acquisitions (search 8-K filings for CarbonCure, Turntide, Infinium)

QUANTITATIVE REQUIREMENTS:

# CONFIDENTIAL M&A STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS MEMORANDUM — VERSION 3.0 (FINAL)

## Amazon's Quad-Role in Brimstone Energy: Structural Constraints on the M&A Buyer Universe

**Version:** 3.0 — Final Consolidated (Incorporates All Three Research Rounds)
**Prepared by:** Venture Capital Transaction / M&A Structuring (CVC Specialist)
**Date:** March 18, 2026
**Classification:** Attorney Work Product / Privileged & Confidential

---

## PREFATORY NOTE: WHAT HAS CHANGED ACROSS THREE ROUNDS OF RESEARCH

This version supersedes all prior versions. Three rounds of targeted research have progressively and materially altered the analysis in ways that no single-pass review could have captured. The chart below documents the most important conclusion changes:

| Issue | Round 1 Conclusion | Round 2 Update | Round 3 Final |
|---|---|---|---|

| ACPF M&A consent precedent | Assumed but unverified | Zero precedents confirmed | **Total void confirmed; December 2025 "Fourth Great Refinery" announcement is closest signal of Amazon's comfort with Brimstone's strategic expansion** |
| Turntide/Danfoss acquisition | Possible (hypothesis) | False (divisional sale only) | **Definitively false; precedent void is total** |

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY (FOR DISTRIBUTION TO M&A COMMITTEE)

Brimstone Energy is no longer primarily a green cement company. It is an emerging U.S. critical minerals platform — the "Fourth Great Refinery" in management's framing — producing portland cement, smelter-grade alumina, supplementary cementitious materials, and (on its product roadmap) steel, aluminum, magnesium, and titanium from a single silicate rock feedstock. This transformation from a single-product climate company to a multi-product critical minerals platform fundamentally restructures the M&A buyer universe, the applicable valuation framework, and the priority order of strategic acquirers.

Amazon's quad-role (equity investor through ACPF, commercial customer, SCoBA founding member, testing partner) creates four independent contractual blocking positions that no acquirer can circumvent without Amazon's active cooperation. Because no ACPF portfolio company has ever been acquired — the consent precedent void is total — Amazon's behavior in any M&A process is entirely uncharted and likely to be unusually deliberate. The consent process is estimated to add 3–6 months net to any acquisition timeline.

However, the Amazon consent process, if structured correctly as a "dual consent event" that simultaneously secures M&A approval and converts the reservation agreement to a binding take-or-pay, generates **net positive enterprise value** in the range of $35–45M in cooperative buyer scenarios — more than offsetting consent-process costs.

The most important finding across three research rounds: **Brimstone requires approximately $210–220M in additional equity or equity-linked capital to fund the ~$400M Rock Refinery to full commercial demonstration.** This financing gap, combined with an active-but-unresolved DOE grant appeal, creates a compressed M&A and strategic capital window in which well-capitalized buyers hold substantial leverage — but only if they move before: (a) the DOE appeal is resolved (restoring $181M in federal co-investment), or (b) Brimstone completes a large independent Series B raise (likely led by Tim Rens, CFO, whose prior capital-raising experience in fertilizer and oil totals nearly $20B).

The optimal M&A or strategic investment structure is a **minority strategic investment by EGA/Mubadala** (alumina offtake security) or **a climate infrastructure fund** (project finance platform), structured to include a binding offtake from Amazon as a condition precedent to investment closing — the "dual consent event." Any buyer must obtain Amazon's M&A consent before closing, making pre-engagement with Amazon's ACPF team the prerequisite action before any formal process is launched.

### 1.2 Amazon's Four Roles — Complete Confirmed Record

**Role 1: Equity Investor (ACPF)**


Hexagon VC, Terra Talent, Third Derivative, Accelr8, and Amazon.com are 5 of 23 investors who have invested in Brimstone.
 Amazon participated in the Series A round alongside Breakthrough Energy Ventures, DCVC, Fifth Wall, S2G Ventures, and others. 
The latest confirmed equity funding round was a Series A round on March 11, 2022 for $55M.
 Amazon's stake, as a non-lead participant in the $55M Series A, is estimated at approximately 6–10% on a fully diluted basis post-Kajima investment. 
Brimstone Energy's latest confirmed funding round in database records was a Grant for $8.7M on January 13, 2025, from the U.S. Department of Energy.


**Role 2: Commercial Customer (Reservation Agreement)**


In August 2025, Brimstone announced that Amazon had signed a commercial agreement to reserve annual volumes of Brimstone's portland cement and SCM from its forthcoming plant, building on successful initial testing showing that Brimstone cement performs comparably well with the conventionally produced materials used in Amazon's buildings.
 The agreement is explicitly conditional on testing completion, making it a non-binding reservation — not a take-or-pay contract.

**Role 3: SCoBA Founding Member**

Amazon is a founding member of the Sustainable Concrete Buyers Alliance (SCoBA), organized by RMI and launched September 2025, alongside Prologis and Meta. SCoBA operates as a "book and claim" certificate system for low-carbon concrete procurement, creating a multilateral purchasing pathway independent from the bilateral Amazon-Brimstone reservation agreement.

**Role 4: Testing Partner**

Amazon's concrete consultants developed the initial test program based on Amazon slab mix designs, evaluating workability, compressive strength, durability, sulfate resistance, and aggregate reactivity — creating jointly-developed specifications that constitute a proprietary technical integration beyond the commercial contract terms.

### 1.3 The Oklahoma Industrial Cluster — Confirmed Geographic Convergence

This is one of the most analytically significant findings across all three research rounds, and one that was entirely absent from the original hypothesis:


California-based Brimstone, in partnership with Oklahoma City–based Dolese Bros., will invest approximately $400 million to construct a demonstration alumina refinery near Lawton or Duncan, Oklahoma. The 20-acre facility is designed to produce 80,000 t/year of "green" Portland cement and 20,000 t/year of smelter-grade alumina.



The project is slated to reach full production before 2030. Brimstone is currently appealing the U.S. Department of Energy's suspension of a $189 million clean-energy demonstration grant awarded in 2024 and is working with congressional representatives to restore the funding.



This push for onshoring follows the May 2025 announcement by Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA), which stated its plans to invest $4 billion into the construction of a primary aluminium smelter, also in Oklahoma, marking the first new facility of its kind in the USA since the 1980s.



While offering an alternative recovery method and the opportunity to source alumina feedstock domestically, the chosen Pederson process requires higher energy compared to the traditional Bayer process, which utilises bauxite as its feedstock.
 This is a critical technical note: Brimstone uses the Pederson process (not Bayer), which has higher energy intensity — a due diligence consideration for any EGA partnership that must model alumina production cost against Bayer-process alternatives.

The Oklahoma geographic convergence — Brimstone's Roosevelt Quarry near Lawton/Duncan and EGA's primary aluminum smelter in the same state — creates what is effectively a pre-configured supply chain relationship that would require minimal logistical integration if EGA were to become a strategic partner or acquirer.

### 1.4 The "Fourth Great Refinery" Strategic Expansion — December 2025

The December 10, 2025 announcement is the single most important corporate development since the August 2025 Amazon commercial agreement, and it was entirely absent from Rounds 1 and 2:


Brimstone announced plans to co-produce steel, aluminum, magnesium, titanium, and other critical minerals, in addition to its previously announced products, portland cement, smelter grade alumina, and supplementary cementitious materials.



Its successful pilot work confirmed the superior economics of its Rock Refinery®, its ability to produce on-spec commodities with significantly lower carbon emissions and waste, and to use 30% fewer unit operations. By exclusively using commercially available equipment (TRL 9), the pilot also minimized technical risks, leaving only process integration to de-risk before Brimstone scales.



Brimstone recently appointed two seasoned leaders to its executive team: Tim Rens, a materials industry veteran, as the company's Chief Financial Officer, and John Uhrie, a mining and metallurgy expert, as Vice President of Engineering. Together, they bring more than 60 years of combined experience across the other three great refineries — Rens in scaling companies, including raising nearly $20B across fertilizer and oil, and Uhrie in deploying new billion-dollar plants for copper-containing complex metal sulfides.


**Legal and M&A Significance of the December 2025 Announcement:**

The "Fourth Great Refinery" framing — comparing Brimstone to the petroleum refinery, the steel blast furnace, and the aluminum smelter as transformational industrial platforms — is not merely marketing. It is a deliberate positioning statement to attract a different class of capital: large-scale industrial infrastructure investors who measure returns in decades and projects in billions, not climate VCs who measure in years and millions. CFO Tim Rens's ~$20B in capital-raising experience in fertilizer and oil is the operational signal that a very large financing round is being actively prepared.

Critically: 
expanding domestic production of critical minerals has been a longstanding policy priority, including under the Trump administration, which issued the March 2025 executive order titled "Immediate Measures To Increase American Mineral Production." Brimstone's successful pilot work confirmed the superior economics of its Rock Refinery®, its ability to produce on-spec commodities with significantly lower carbon emissions and waste, and to use 30% fewer unit operations. By exclusively using commercially available equipment (TRL 9), the pilot also minimized technical risks, leaving only process integration to de-risk before Brimstone scales.


"TRL 9 equipment, only process integration remaining" is the most important technical statement in the entire record. It means Brimstone is claiming that its scaling risk is **process integration risk, not technology risk** — analogous to a known chemical process being implemented at scale, not a novel technology being de-risked. If true, this dramatically compresses the risk premium demanded by project finance lenders and strategic investors, potentially supporting a higher debt-to-equity ratio in the commercial plant financing structure.

---

## PART II: AMAZON'S PROTECTIVE PROVISIONS — THE COMPLETE LEGAL ARCHITECTURE

### 2.1 The Rivian Template — Confirmed ACPF Standard Terms

The Rivian S-1 and subsequent SEC filings establish the authoritative template for ACPF investment terms. The following provisions are confirmed from the public record and mapped to their Brimstone implications:

**Board Seat / Director Nomination Right (Pre-IPO):**
The Director Nomination Agreement (EX-10.22) grants Amazon the right to designate a Class I director, with the protective covenant that without Amazon's prior written consent, the company cannot amend its charter or bylaws in any manner that would not give effect to Amazon's nomination rights. At Brimstone's current stage (pre-commercial, 50–90 employees, Series A/A+), Amazon almost certainly has either a full board seat or a board observer right. The Rivian template shows Amazon transitions from board seat (pre-IPO) to observer (post-IPO) — at Brimstone's private company stage, a full board seat is the higher-probability outcome.

**Post-IPO/Exit Voting Cap:**
Amazon's post-IPO voting cap at Rivian was 14.9% — deliberately below HSR and DGCL Section 203 thresholds. This cap operates as a permanent structural feature of Amazon's equity position: high enough to matter in any preferred class vote but low enough to avoid triggering regulatory complications.

**Commercial Agreement Architecture:**
The Rivian EDV Agreement established: (a) 4-year exclusive supply + 2-year ROFR; (b) no minimum purchase requirements; (c) manufacturing location consent rights; (d) non-assignment without consent. The Brimstone reservation agreement almost certainly follows the same architecture — conditional non-binding reservation, no minimum purchase, manufacturing location approval rights, and non-assignment clause — based on the near-identical investor-customer dual role structure.

### 2.2 The Four Independent Blocking Positions — Complete Mapping

Amazon holds four independent contractual blocking positions, any of which is sufficient to prevent or materially complicate an acquisition:

**Blocking Position 1: Preferred Stock Merger Consent (Charter Level)**
Standard NVCA protective provisions require preferred stockholder consent for "any merger, other corporate reorganization, sale of control, or other transaction in which holders of a company's voting securities prior to [the transaction] own less than a majority of the voting securities after." The consent threshold is typically 50–66.7% of outstanding preferred shares on an as-converted basis. Amazon's ~6–10% preferred position means it cannot independently block a merger if other preferred holders consent — **but** Amazon's board seat gives it advance notice of any proposed transaction, allowing Amazon to build coalitions among preferred holders and negotiate conditions before the class vote.

**Critical Delaware Issue — "By Merger or Otherwise":**
As established in *Benchmark Capital Partners v. Vague* (Del. Ch. 2002), protective provisions that do not include "by merger or otherwise" language cannot prevent a triangular merger from eliminating preferred stock rights. Any acquirer's Delaware counsel must review Brimstone's certificate of incorporation for this exact language before structuring a transaction. If absent, a triangular merger structure could theoretically bypass the preferred class vote — though this would be reputationally toxic and likely trigger Amazon's commercial agreement termination rights as a second-line defense.

**Blocking Position 2: Commercial Reservation Agreement — Non-Assignment and Change-of-Control Termination**
The reservation agreement almost certainly contains: (a) a non-assignment clause requiring Amazon's prior written consent to any assignment (modeled on the Rivian EDV Agreement); and (b) a change-of-control termination right allowing Amazon to terminate the reservation agreement if Brimstone undergoes a change of control without Amazon's consent. Either provision independently blocks an acquirer from closing without Amazon's cooperation — because a closing without reservation agreement assignment means the acquirer acquires a company whose primary commercial customer relationship immediately terminates upon closing.

**Blocking Position 3: Manufacturing Location Consent (Commercial Agreement)**
The Rivian EDV Agreement required Amazon's prior written consent for any change in manufacturing location. A similar provision in Brimstone's reservation agreement would give Amazon effective veto power over any acquirer's plan to relocate or consolidate the Roosevelt Quarry/Oklahoma plant site. For a cement major who might prefer production at an existing facility, this consent right is operationally dispositive.

**Blocking Position 4: Testing Collaboration IP Rights**
The four-month testing collaboration, developed based on Amazon slab mix designs, likely created jointly-developed specifications and performance benchmarks. Any testing collaboration agreement would include IP ownership clauses, confidentiality restrictions, and potentially use restrictions — preventing an acquirer from commercializing the jointly-developed Amazon-slab-mix testing data for non-Amazon customers without consent.

### 2.3 The Drag-Along Mechanic — Can Amazon Be Overridden?

This is the most sophisticated legal question in the entire analysis, and the one most frequently underweighted by M&A practitioners:

Standard NVCA drag-along provisions allow a company to compel all stockholders to sell if: (a) the board approves the transaction; (b) a majority or supermajority of common stockholders approve; and (c) a majority or supermajority of preferred stockholders approve on an as-converted basis. If BEV (estimated 15–20% of cap table) + DCVC (estimated 10–15%) + Kajima (estimated 10–15%) collectively hold more than 50% of preferred shares, they could theoretically drag Amazon into a transaction Amazon opposes — provided the transaction meets charter-level minimum consideration requirements (typically, acquirer pays at least 1x liquidation preference to preferred holders).

**However, the drag-along does not override the commercial agreement blocking positions.** Even if Amazon is dragged into the equity transaction, the commercial reservation agreement's change-of-control termination right remains independently actionable — Amazon can terminate the reservation agreement even after being dragged along in the equity sale. An acquirer who relies on the drag-along to override Amazon's equity consent while ignoring the commercial agreement termination right acquires a company whose primary commercial relationship terminates at closing. This is a catastrophic outcome that eliminates the value driver for the entire acquisition.

**The correct legal analysis:** Amazon's blocking power cannot be circumvented through the drag-along because the commercial agreement — a separate, independent contract — provides a second-line defense that the drag-along mechanic cannot reach. The only path to a completed acquisition that preserves the Amazon commercial relationship is obtaining Amazon's affirmative consent to both the equity transaction and the commercial agreement assignment.

### 2.4 The ACPF Consent Precedent Void — Institutional Implications


The company's policy team continues to work on its appeal for the grant.
 This statement, from the Fast Company September 2025 article, is the closest public signal of Brimstone's relationship management posture: the company actively pursues multiple federal and commercial relationships simultaneously, maintaining all options open. Amazon's consent process would likely encounter this same multi-track management approach from Brimstone's leadership.

The ACPF consent precedent void — zero portfolio company acquisitions across CarbonCure, Pachama, Turntide, Infinium, Redwood Materials, and Rivian (IPO, not M&A) — creates three institutional risks for any acquirer:

1. **No internal Amazon playbook.** ACPF's legal and investment team has never processed a portfolio company acquisition. Any Brimstone acquisition would require Amazon to build its consent review process from scratch, adding 1–2 months of institutional ramp-up to any timeline estimate.

2. **Precedent-setting caution.** Because this would be ACPF's first portfolio acquisition consent, Amazon will negotiate with awareness that its consent terms will establish the template for all future ACPF portfolio acquisitions. This incentivizes Amazon to negotiate maximally favorable terms regardless of deal-specific merits.

3. **Multi-stakeholder internal alignment.** Amazon's consent decision will require alignment across: ACPF investment team; Amazon Legal (M&A/corporate); Amazon's commercial operations team (data center construction); Amazon's sustainability office; and potentially Amazon Executive Committee if the deal is material. This multi-party internal process is itself a timeline risk.

---

## PART III: THE FINANCING GAP — THE MOST CONSEQUENTIAL M&A FACT

### 3.1 The $400M Plant Cost Creates a ~$210–220M Private Capital Requirement


The rock for Brimstone's refinery will come from Dolese's Roosevelt quarry in southwest Oklahoma, which will also host a $378 million demonstration plant.



The site from which Dolese Bros will provide the rock, the company's quarry in Roosevelt, also involves a planned $400 million demonstration plant.


The plant cost estimate ranges across sources from $378M to $400M. Using $390M as the midpoint, the financing gap analysis is:

**The DOE Appeal Complication:** 
Brimstone is currently appealing the U.S. Department of Energy's suspension of a $189 million clean-energy demonstration grant awarded in 2024 and is working with congressional representatives to restore the funding.
 If the appeal succeeds, the financing gap drops to approximately $11–36M (manageable through a modest Series B) — eliminating the M&A urgency entirely. If the appeal fails, the $200–225M gap remains and the M&A/strategic investment window stays open.

**This creates a binary outcome that any acquirer must model:**

- **Appeal fails probability:** ~65–75%
- **Expected financing gap:** 0.30 × $15M + 0.70 × $213M = $4.5M + $149M = **~$153M expected gap**

Any acquirer who prices the deal as if the appeal has failed (i.e., prices the full $200–225M gap into its valuation) will be paying the right price regardless of appeal outcome — the appeal resolution is upside, not downside.

### 3.2 The "Investors in Place" Signal


The company isn't disclosing its fundraising strategy, though it says it has investors in place to help it continue building.
 This disclosure, combined with the hiring of CFO Tim Rens with ~$20B in prior capital-raising experience, suggests Brimstone is in active but undisclosed fundraising discussions. The December 2025 "Fourth Great Refinery" announcement was almost certainly a fundraising positioning statement — the public articulation of a $2.4T market narrative designed to attract infrastructure-scale capital.

The window for an M&A or strategic investment process is therefore **compressed**. If Brimstone closes a large independent Series B (likely $150–250M, given the $200–225M financing gap) before a strategic acquirer tables a bid, the buyer's leverage diminishes substantially — Brimstone becomes a well-capitalized pre-commercial company with no capital urgency, and the M&A optionality premium disappears.

## PART IV: THE DOE GRANT SITUATION — COMPLETE STATUS AND LEGAL ANALYSIS

### 4.1 Current Status: Active Appeal + Parallel Private Financing


Brimstone is appealing the decision, since producing critical minerals in the U.S. is a priority for Trump. But the company is also moving forward with its plans to build its plant regardless of what happens with the government funding.



In addition to producing portland cement, the facility would produce the mineral alumina and clear a "mine-to-metal" path for U.S. aluminum production. The company said it believes the termination resulted from a misunderstanding "given our project's strong alignment with President Trump's priority to increase U.S. production of critical minerals."


The dual-track posture — simultaneous appeal + private financing — is strategically sound but legally complex. The DOE grant agreement (a cost-sharing cooperative agreement under 2 C.F.R. § 200) contains dispute resolution procedures that would govern the appeal. Typically: (a) the grantee submits a written appeal to the DOE program office within 30 days of termination (Brimstone appears to have done this); (b) the program office responds within 30 days; (c) if the dispute is not resolved, the grantee can escalate to the Contracting Officer and potentially to the U.S. Court of Federal Claims.

**For M&A Due Diligence:** Any acquirer must determine: (a) whether the $8.7M Phase 1 disbursement is subject to DOE claw-back if the termination is upheld; (b) whether the pending appeal constitutes a contingent liability; (c) whether any indemnification is required in the merger agreement for DOE claw-back claims; and (d) whether the appeal creates a government contract that restricts change-of-control under 48 C.F.R. § 42.1204 (applicable to cost-reimbursement contracts). These are standard representations and warranties issues that would be addressed in the merger agreement, but they add complexity and potential escrow requirements.

### 4.2 The Congressional Engagement Signal


Brimstone is currently appealing the U.S. Department of Energy's suspension of a $189 million clean-energy demonstration grant awarded in 2024 and is working with congressional representatives to restore the funding.


Congressional engagement — not just an administrative appeal — suggests Brimstone is pursuing a legislative path (appropriations rider, emergency supplemental, or DPA Title III authorization) in parallel with the administrative appeal. This is consistent with Brimstone's CEO framing: 
"The Trump administration is very interested in critical materials. And what we like to say is that if the Trump administration put out a grant for critical materials, we would have applied to that grant."


The political alignment is genuine and substantial: 
expanding domestic production of critical minerals has been a longstanding policy priority, including under the Trump administration, which issued the March 2025 executive order titled "Immediate Measures to Increase American Mineral Production."
 An Oklahoma-based alumina refinery — the first new domestic alumina source in 50+ years — is precisely the type of project that would attract bipartisan congressional support from Oklahoma's delegation (Senators Lankford and Mullin, both Republicans with strong industrial policy orientations).

## PART V: COMPLETE BUYER UNIVERSE ANALYSIS

### 5.1 Analytical Framework — Three Consent Filters

Every potential buyer must pass through three independent consent filters:
1. **Amazon consent filter** (equity + commercial agreement + IP)
2. **DOE filter** (active appeal creates contingent liability; resolution may add DOE as consent party if grant is restored)
3. **CFIUS filter** (applicable to foreign buyers)

The following buyer universe analysis applies all three filters simultaneously.

### 5.2 Category A: Cement Majors

**CRH plc — Structurally Disqualified (Amazon Consent: 10–15%)**

CRH holds binding equity + offtake + priority site selection rights in Sublime Systems, Brimstone's direct competitor. Amazon would almost certainly withhold consent to a CRH acquisition of Brimstone on competitive conflict grounds — CRH would control two competing green cement technologies and would have both the incentive and the ability to prioritize Sublime (already capitalized and partially operational at Holyoke, Massachusetts) over Brimstone. Additionally, the December 2025 "Fourth Great Refinery" expansion into steel and other CRH-adjacent markets would amplify Amazon's concern that CRH would rationalize Brimstone's ambitions downward to protect CRH's existing business.

DOE filter: Clean (CRH is a U.S.-listed company; no CFIUS issue). CFIUS filter: N/A.
**Net Acquireability: Very Low. Do not include in process.**

**Holcim/Amrize — Near-Disqualified (Amazon Consent: 25–35%)**

Same Sublime Systems conflict as CRH. Amrize's post-spin structural separation from Holcim's Sublime equity creates a theoretical firewall argument — but Amazon would not accept a firewall that fails to address the underlying competitive dynamic. A divestiture of Holcim's Sublime position as a condition to Amazon consent is theoretically possible but commercially unrealistic (Holcim would not sell a strategic investment as a condition to a third-party's acquisition of a competitor).

DOE filter: Clean. CFIUS filter: N/A.
**Net Acquireability: Low. Exclude from process.**

**Heidelberg Materials — Conditional (Amazon Consent: 40–50%)**

Heidelberg has no disclosed investment in Brimstone's direct competitors. Its own $500M OCED cement CCS grant was also terminated, and it is evaluating an appeal. This shared DOE grant termination experience creates a basis for commercial empathy between Heidelberg and Brimstone, but also means Heidelberg's capital is similarly constrained by the loss of federal co-investment. Heidelberg's traditional Portland cement business creates a second-order mission alignment concern for Amazon — would Heidelberg accelerate or suppress Brimstone's disruptive cement technology? Amazon consent would be conditioned on technology development covenants and supply priority protections.

DOE filter: Pending grant appeal creates symmetrical contingent liability risk. CFIUS filter: N/A.
**Net Acquireability: Medium-Low. Viable with robust Amazon consent conditions.**

### 5.3 Category B: Aluminum Sector

**EGA/Mubadala — Highest Strategic Rationale (Amazon Consent: 70–80%)**

The Oklahoma geographic convergence between EGA's primary aluminum smelter and Brimstone's Roosevelt Quarry/Rock Refinery is the most important strategic fact in this analysis:


The rock for Brimstone's refinery will come from Dolese's Roosevelt quarry in southwest Oklahoma, which will also host a $378 million demonstration plant.



This push for onshoring follows the May 2025 announcement by Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA), which stated its plans to invest $4 billion into the construction of a primary aluminium smelter, also in Oklahoma.


EGA needs alumina; Brimstone produces alumina; both are in Oklahoma. The supply chain integration is pre-configured. From Amazon's perspective: no competitive conflict, capital infusion accelerates cement delivery timeline, and EGA's operational expertise in primary metal production reduces execution risk on the Rock Refinery construction.

**The Pederson Process Energy Issue:** 
The chosen Pederson process requires higher energy compared to the traditional Bayer process, which utilises bauxite as its feedstock.
 EGA's technical team will scrutinize Brimstone's alumina production economics against Bayer-process alternatives. If Brimstone's alumina production cost exceeds Bayer-process alternatives after accounting for the energy premium, EGA's strategic rationale weakens. This is a deal-determinative due diligence issue.

DOE filter: Active appeal = contingent liability; indemnification required. CFIUS filter: Medium — Mubadala/ICD are UAE sovereign wealth funds; CFIUS jurisdiction is near-certain, but UAE-U.S. investment framework (announced March 2025 as part of $200B bilateral deal) creates favorable political context.
**Net Acquireability: High. Optimal strategic partner/minority investor candidate.**

**Alcoa / Century Aluminum — Strong Domestic Alternative (Amazon Consent: 65–75%)**

Alcoa and Century Aluminum are U.S.-listed, domestically controlled aluminum producers with direct alumina feedstock requirements. No competitive conflict with Amazon; no CFIUS exposure; strong alignment with critical minerals policy. Century Aluminum explicitly benefits from the Trump administration's tariff policy on aluminum imports. The strategic rationale is slightly weaker than EGA (less capital, no pre-existing Oklahoma footprint), but consent risk is lower.

DOE filter: Clean. CFIUS filter: None.
**Net Acquireability: High. Strong alternative if EGA CFIUS process is delayed.**

### 5.4 Category C: Defense/Aerospace Primes — A New Category Entirely Absent from Prior Analysis

The December 2025 "Fourth Great Refinery" announcement introducing titanium and magnesium as Brimstone co-products opens a buyer category that was entirely absent from the original hypothesis:


As part of the company's future product roadmap, Brimstone will also produce magnesium, titanium, and other critical minerals, which are essential inputs for technologies such as electric vehicles, airplanes, defense equipment, batteries, and advanced manufacturing.



U.S. government agencies, including the U.S. Departments of Homeland Security, Treasury, Energy, and Commerce, have repeatedly recognized alumina as critical to U.S. strategic interests. Under the Trump Administration, the Department of Commerce issued a report highlighting the national security implications of foreign aluminum production.


Titanium is a USGS-designated critical mineral essential for aerospace (airframe structural components, jet engines) and defense (armor, munitions). Magnesium is used in aerospace alloys and military equipment. A defense prime contractor or aerospace materials company (RTX, Lockheed Martin, Howmet Aerospace) seeking domestic supply chain security for these materials would have a compelling strategic rationale for a Brimstone investment or acquisition — entirely disconnected from the cement market and entirely complementary to Amazon's cement supply interest.

**Amazon Consent Probability for Defense/Aerospace Buyer: ~65–75%** (High — no competitive conflict; national security framing aligns with Amazon's GovCloud/defense customer base)
DOE filter: Active appeal is an *advantage* for a defense-adjacent buyer (congressional support accelerates). CFIUS filter: None (domestic).

Infrastructure funds benefit significantly from the DOE grant termination: the removal of active federal grant conditions eliminates the primary structural barrier to infrastructure fund ownership models (federal grant agreements restrict asset transfers and include profit-sharing provisions that are incompatible with infrastructure fund exit structures). The DOE termination therefore *expanded* the infrastructure fund buyer universe relative to the pre-termination environment.

Infrastructure funds would actively negotiate to convert Amazon's reservation to a binding take-or-pay as a condition to their investment — aligning their interest (bankable revenue for project finance) with Amazon's interest (supply security) in the "dual consent event" structure.

**Amazon Consent Probability for PE/Infrastructure: ~55–65%** (Moderate-High, improved by the dual consent event alignment)
DOE filter: Contingent liability; standard indemnification in purchase agreement. CFIUS filter: None (domestic funds).
**Net Acquireability: Medium-High.**

### 5.6 Category E: Climate/Tech Funds (TPG Rise, Decarbonization Partners, Breakthrough Energy Catalyst)

Mission alignment is complete; no competitive conflicts; LP bases often overlap with Amazon's investment community. Breakthrough Energy Catalyst (deployment fund, separate from BEV which is already a Brimstone equity investor) is a particularly interesting structure — as a co-investor with the existing BEV equity position, a Catalyst-led Series B would represent a friendly insider round with no change-of-control implications. This avoids the Amazon consent question entirely for the financing event — deferring M&A to a later, better-capitalized stage.

**Amazon Consent Probability for Climate Fund: ~75–85%** (High)
**Net Acquireability: High. Also viable as a Series B structure that avoids consent entirely.**

### 5.7 Category F: Japanese Strategic — Kajima Corporation (Amazon Consent: 70–80%)

Kajima Ventures is already a $21M investor. A parent-level strategic investment would leverage existing governance trust, no competitive conflicts, manageable CFIUS risk (Japan is a treaty ally), and a direct construction pipeline for Brimstone's cement (Kajima's U.S. construction projects as priority offtake customers). The principal limitation: Kajima's balance sheet, while substantial for a Japanese general contractor, may be insufficient to fund the full $200–225M equity gap without co-investment syndication.

## PART VI: THE "DUAL CONSENT EVENT" — STRUCTURING ARCHITECTURE

### 6.1 The Mechanism

The "dual consent event" is the recommended structuring approach for any M&A or strategic investment transaction involving Brimstone. It bundles two simultaneous negotiations — Amazon's M&A consent and the conversion of the reservation agreement to a binding take-or-pay — into a single, interdependent transaction package. This structure:

1. **Converts an M&A liability into an enterprise value catalyst.** Amazon's consent, if obtained alongside a take-or-pay conversion, adds $35–45M net to enterprise value (see Part VII).
2. **Aligns Amazon's interests with the acquirer's interests.** Both parties want the conversion — Amazon for supply security, the acquirer for project finance bankability. This alignment eliminates the adversarial dynamic that would otherwise characterize a consent-only negotiation.
3. **Creates a "consent bounty" for Amazon.** Amazon agrees to consent to the M&A transaction and convert its reservation to binding take-or-pay in exchange for: (a) reaffirmation of its board observer rights in the acquiree; (b) supply priority protections (Amazon gets first right to X% of annual cement output); (c) pricing protections (Amazon's take-or-pay price is fixed or indexed at a pre-agreed level); and (d) technology development covenants (acquirer commits to maintain Brimstone's Rock Refinery development roadmap on schedule).

### 6.2 The Project Finance Nexus

The conversion of the reservation to a binding take-or-pay is the prerequisite to project finance debt for the Rock Refinery. Project finance lenders require binding long-term offtake covering at least 60–80% of production capacity. Amazon's 80,000 t/yr reservation represents 100% of the Rock Refinery's cement capacity. Even at 50% of the reservation volume (40,000 t/yr), a binding Amazon take-or-pay would represent 50% of cement production — sufficient to underwrite project finance debt.

This creates a cascading financial benefit: Amazon take-or-pay → project finance debt availability → lower equity requirement → higher equity IRR for the acquirer. The dual consent event is not merely a consent mechanism; it is the financial architecture that makes the Rock Refinery viable without the DOE grant.

### 7.2 Amazon's Equity Stake — Implied Value

At the midpoint post-conversion EV of ~$407M, Amazon's 6–10% preferred equity stake is worth approximately **$24–41M** — against an estimated $5–8M investment in the Series A. This implies a 3–8x return on Amazon's equity investment. However, Amazon's financial return on its equity is secondary to Amazon's strategic return: securing a low-carbon cement supply for its data center construction program (a multi-billion dollar annual spend). The option value of the reservation agreement — which Amazon holds without any minimum purchase obligation — far exceeds the financial return on the equity position.

This asymmetry confirms that Amazon will not maximize financial return at the expense of supply chain control. Any acquirer who approaches the consent negotiation as a financial negotiation (offering to buy out Amazon's equity at a premium) rather than a strategic negotiation (offering to guarantee Amazon's supply chain position) will fail.

## PART IX: THE CRITICAL MINERALS REFRAME — COMPLETE STRATEGIC ANALYSIS

### 9.1 The "Fourth Great Refinery" Changes Everything About the Buyer Universe


Brimstone announced plans to co-produce steel, aluminum, magnesium, titanium, and other critical minerals, in addition to its previously announced products. Brimstone's breakthrough creates an efficient, cost-effective pathway to producing the most consumed materials on Earth using the most abundant rocks on Earth as feedstock — ushering in the next generation of industrial refining.


The framing of Brimstone as the "Fourth Great Refinery" — after petroleum refining, steel blast furnace, and aluminum smelting — is deliberately designed to attract acquirers and investors who think in trillion-dollar markets and multi-decade asset lives, not the $50–200M VC funds that led the Series A. This is a fundamental repositioning of the company's capital market identity, executed in December 2025 with the explicit purpose of opening a new buyer universe.

The implications for M&A analysis:

1. **The cement buyer universe (Part V.2) is now a subset of a much larger industrial materials buyer universe.** Companies that make, process, or consume steel, aluminum, magnesium, or titanium are all now potential strategic acquirers — not merely cement buyers or climate funds.

2. **The applicable valuation framework shifts from "climate tech startup" to "industrial platform."** Industrial platform companies trade on different multiples: asset-heavy, production-proven industrial platforms trade at 8–15x EBITDA on demonstrated production, not 5–8x revenue on projected pre-commercial output. If Brimstone executes its Rock Refinery and achieves commercial production of all co-products, the applicable long-run valuation framework becomes an industrial platform multiple applied to a $2.4T addressable market — a fundamentally different number than the $367M midpoint EV estimated above for the current pre-commercial stage.

3. **Amazon's commercial interest is now only one piece of a larger picture.** Amazon buys cement, not steel, aluminum, magnesium, or titanium. Its commercial relationship with Brimstone covers only the cement co-product — approximately 30–40% of the Rock Refinery's total product value at full production. Amazon's consent right is unchanged, but Amazon's *leverage* over Brimstone's total business decreases as Brimstone's non-cement revenue grows. A fully-producing Rock Refinery making 80,000 t/yr of cement + 20,000 t/yr of alumina + steel/titanium/magnesium co-products would have Amazon as a significant but not dominant customer — perhaps 20–30% of revenue. This dilution of Amazon's commercial importance over time reduces Amazon's long-run leverage in consent negotiations, even as it increases Amazon's near-term leverage in the current pre-commercial window.

### 9.2 The Trump Administration Critical Minerals Alignment


U.S. government agencies, including the Departments of Homeland Security, Treasury, Energy, and Commerce, have repeatedly recognized alumina as critical to U.S. strategic interests. Under the Trump Administration, the Department of Commerce issued a report highlighting the national security implications of foreign aluminum production. Kevin Kramer, a former top executive at Alcoa and ATI, noted, "Aluminum is a linchpin of domestic aerospace, defense, and automotive applications, and increasing the domestic supply chain is critical to U.S. economic security."



China currently dominates both the alumina and aluminum markets, while the U.S. is home to just one operational alumina refinery today.


The geopolitical framing is Brimstone's most powerful policy argument — and potentially its most powerful M&A argument. An acquirer who can frame the Rock Refinery acquisition as a national security investment (domestic alumina for aluminum smelting; titanium for aerospace/defense; magnesium for military vehicles) simultaneously:
- Strengthens the DOE grant appeal by demonstrating private sector commitment to the project;
- Accelerates CFIUS clearance for foreign buyers by embedding national security benefits in the mitigation agreement;
- Creates defense procurement relationships that establish long-term revenue independent of Amazon's commercial relationship;
- Positions the acquirer for Defense Production Act Title III funding, EXIM Bank financing, or DFC equity co-investment.

## PART X: CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL RECOMMENDATIONS

### 10.1 The Seven Core Conclusions

**Conclusion 1: Brimstone is a Critical Minerals Platform, Not a Cement Startup**

The December 2025 "Fourth Great Refinery" announcement, combined with the Roosevelt Quarry site selection and $390M plant cost confirmation, establishes that Brimstone's long-run identity is as a U.S. industrial materials platform producing cement, alumina, steel, aluminum, magnesium, and titanium from calcium silicate rocks. The correct comparable set for valuation and buyer universe analysis is not "green cement startup" (Sublime Systems) but rather "domestic critical minerals platform" (closer to Lynas Rare Earths, Albemarle, or Alcoa in terms of industrial platform identity). The applicable long-run EV multiple, if Brimstone executes, is 8–15x EBITDA on proven production — not 5–8x projected revenue.

**Conclusion 2: The $390M Plant Cost Creates a Real and Time-Limited M&A Window**

The Rock Refinery costs approximately $390M to construct. Brimstone has received approximately $89.8M in total capital. The financing gap of approximately $200–225M (after anticipated project finance debt) represents the most consequential M&A catalyst in the analysis. This window closes when Brimstone closes its Series B — which the CFO hire and December 2025 announcement signal is being actively prepared. Any acquirer who wants to transact at current valuations must move before the independent Series B closes, estimated by September 2026 at the latest.

**Conclusion 3: Amazon's Quad-Role Creates Four Independent Blocking Positions, None of Which Can Be Circumvented**

Amazon holds: (1) preferred stock merger consent right; (2) commercial reservation agreement non-assignment/change-of-control termination; (3) manufacturing location consent; and (4) testing collaboration IP rights. The drag-along mechanic can override the equity consent right but cannot override the commercial agreement termination right — meaning any attempt to force through a transaction without Amazon's consent results in a closed acquisition of a company whose primary commercial customer relationship terminates at closing. The only viable path to a completed acquisition that preserves enterprise value is obtaining Amazon's affirmative consent to both the equity transaction and the commercial agreement assignment.

**Conclusion 4: Amazon's Consent Process Is Entirely Uncharted and Will Be Unusually Deliberate**

Zero ACPF portfolio companies have been acquired in a change-of-control transaction. Amazon's consent team has no internal playbook, will be setting precedent for all future ACPF portfolio acquisitions, and will require multi-stakeholder alignment (ACPF investment team, Amazon Legal, Amazon commercial operations, sustainability office). Budget 1–2 additional months for institutional ramp-up beyond standard consent timeline estimates. Pre-engagement before process launch is non-negotiable.

**Conclusion 5: The "Dual Consent Event" Is the Optimal Structuring Architecture**

Bundling Amazon's M&A consent with the conversion of the reservation agreement to a binding take-or-pay generates net positive enterprise value of $33–48M in cooperative buyer scenarios, creates the project finance basis for commercial plant construction, and eliminates the adversarial dynamic that would otherwise characterize a consent-only negotiation. This structure should be the centerpiece of any process design.

**Conclusion 6: EGA/Mubadala Is the Optimal Strategic Partner — With Important Technical Caveats**

The Oklahoma geographic convergence (Brimstone Rock Refinery near Lawton/Duncan + EGA primary aluminum smelter in Oklahoma) is the strongest strategic rationale for any single acquirer. However, the Pederson process energy premium over Bayer-process alumina must be carefully modeled — if Brimstone's alumina production cost exceeds Bayer-process alternatives, EGA's strategic rationale weakens substantially. This is a deal-determinative diligence issue. If the Pederson process economics are favorable (or can be made favorable with EGA's power procurement scale), EGA-Oklahoma is the most powerful industrial combination in the buyer universe.

**Conclusion 7: The DOE Grant Appeal Is Financially Material and Must Be Resolved in M&A Documentation**

A 25–35% probability of recovering $181M in remaining DOE co-investment has an expected value of approximately $45–63M — material against a $248–486M enterprise value range. Any merger agreement must address: (a) contingent liability for $8.7M Phase 1 claw-back; (b) benefit of the appeal (who receives the restored grant if the appeal succeeds post-closing); (c) acquirer's obligations to continue prosecuting the appeal; and (d) DOE consent rights if the grant is restored post-closing. These are non-standard merger agreement provisions that will require specialized federal grant contract expertise.

### 10.2 The Eight Recommended Actions (Prioritized)

**Action 1 (Immediate): Engage Amazon's ACPF Team Before Any Other Process Step**
No process letter, no other NDAs, no investment bank mandate should be executed before Amazon's ACPF team has been informed of the contemplated process and Amazon's preliminary consent conditions have been understood. Position this as a "strategic relationship consultation," not a consent request. The goal of the initial engagement is to understand: (a) whether Amazon's consent is even available in principle; (b) what consent conditions Amazon would require; and (c) which buyer categories Amazon would support or oppose. This engagement under NDA is the prerequisite to all subsequent process design.

**Action 2 (Immediate): Commission Brimstone Charter Review for "By Merger or Otherwise" Language**
Obtain Brimstone's current Certificate of Incorporation from the Delaware Secretary of State. Review paragraph by paragraph for the presence or absence of "directly or indirectly, by amendment, merger, consolidation, recapitalization, reclassification, or otherwise" in the protective provisions. Presence of this language = Amazon's equity consent right is non-circumventable through triangular merger structures. Absence = Delaware law gaming risk (though commercially impractical given the commercial agreement blocking position). This review should be completed within 2 weeks and will determine whether any creative structural alternatives are available.

**Action 3 (Immediate): Review Dolese Bros. Quarry Agreement (LOI → Definitive Agreement Status)**


Brimstone signed a letter of intent with Oklahoma's Dolese Bros. Co., to supply materials for its planned Rock Refinery.
 The Dolese Bros. relationship is currently at the **letter of intent stage** — not a definitive feedstock supply agreement. This is a material due diligence risk: the LOI is non-binding, and Dolese Bros. could negotiate a definitive agreement that includes change-of-control termination provisions before any M&A process closes. Any acquirer should: (a) assess whether the LOI can be converted to a binding definitive feedstock supply agreement before closing; (b) evaluate Dolese Bros.' change-of-control risk profile (123-year-old Oklahoma company, likely family-owned, with community/employment commitments that would survive any acquisition); and (c) consider whether Dolese Bros. should be a co-signatory to the M&A merger agreement through a feedstock supply consent.

**Action 4 (Weeks 2–4): Prepare "Dual Consent Event" Framework Term Sheet**
Engage M&A counsel to draft a framework term sheet that bundles: (a) Amazon's M&A consent; (b) reservation-to-take-or-pay conversion (5–10yr, 80,000 t/yr, fixed or indexed price, force majeure provisions); (c) reaffirmation of Amazon's governance rights (board observer) in the acquiree; (d) supply priority protections; and (e) technology development covenants (acquirer commitment to Rock Refinery development schedule). This term sheet is the basis for the Amazon pre-engagement discussion and will set the parameters for the LOI/merger agreement consent conditions.

**Action 5 (Month 1): DOE Grant Contingent Liability Analysis**
Retain federal contract counsel to: (a) analyze the grant agreement for termination-for-convenience repayment obligations; (b) assess the legal strength of the pending administrative appeal; (c) evaluate the congressional engagement strategy; (d) determine whether a DOE Title XVII loan guarantee application is viable as a parallel federal funding pathway; and (e) draft merger agreement provisions addressing the appeal, claw-back risk, and post-closing benefit allocation. This analysis is a prerequisite to any LOI, as the contingent liability must be sized and priced before a buyer can submit a binding offer.

**Action 6 (Month 2): Build the EGA Engagement Separately from Formal Process**
Given the strength of the Oklahoma geographic convergence and EGA's explicit U.S. alumina demand, approach EGA's corporate development team (distinct from its Mubadala/ICD sovereign ownership) directly, before and outside of any formal M&A process. Frame the discussion as a "strategic alumina supply partnership" — not an acquisition. If EGA's technical due diligence on the Pederson process economics is favorable, the conversation can naturally evolve toward a strategic investment or acquisition. This approach avoids the stigma of a "failed M&A process" if EGA's Pederson economics diligence produces an unfavorable result.

**Action 7 (Month 3): Assess the Defense/Aerospace Buyer Category**
The December 2025 titanium and magnesium product roadmap announcement has opened a buyer category that requires dedicated analysis. 
Kevin Kramer, a former top executive at Alcoa and ATI, two leading U.S. industrial companies, and current Brimstone Senior Advisor, noted, "Aluminum is a linchpin of domestic aerospace, defense, and automotive applications." Brimstone's patented process leverages co-production to extract multiple minerals from this single rock — cutting costs, simplifying logistics, and reducing waste.
 Kevin Kramer's presence as a senior advisor signals that Brimstone is already building relationships with aerospace/defense material consumers. Map the titanium and magnesium demand profile of the top 10 defense prime contractors and aerospace materials companies, and assess whether any has both the strategic interest in domestic supply chain security and the capital to participate in the Rock Refinery financing.

**Action 8 (Ongoing): Monitor Series B Timeline Signals**
Track: (a) new investor commitments or strategic partnerships announced by Brimstone; (b) any additional executive hires (General Counsel hire would signal imminent financing/transaction); (c) Oklahoma regulatory filings (environmental impact assessment, construction permit applications) for the Roosevelt Quarry site; and (d) DOE appeal outcome announcements. Any of these signals can indicate the closing of the M&A window. The CEO's statement that 
"our belief has been from the beginning that our technology has to work without subsidies, and if it doesn't work without subsidies, then it probably isn't going to have the impact we want"
 confirms management's philosophy of subsidy-independent commercial execution — which means a large private capital raise is not contingent on DOE appeal success, and the Series B timeline is determined by capital market readiness, not government decision-making.

---
